mnemonics.title: SPAKE
mnemonics.content: SPAKE = Simple Password-Authenticated Key Exchange. Remember: 'S'imple 'P'assword 'A'uthenticated 'K'ey 'E'xchange - a protocol where two parties authenticate each other using a shared password without revealing the password itself.

mnemonics.title: 3FA
mnemonics.content: Three Authentication Factors: 'K'now (password/PIN), 'H'ave (token/smartphone), 'B'e (biometric). Remember: 'KHB' - Keep Hands Busy authenticating!

mnemonics.title: Oauth2
mnemonics.content: OAuth 2.0 Flow: 'R'equest → 'A'pprove → 'G'rant Token → 'A'ccess Resource. Remember: 'RAGA' - Request Approval Gets Access!

mnemonics.title: OID Connect
mnemonics.content: OpenID Connect = OAuth 2.0 + Identity Layer. Remember: 'O'auth + 'I'Dentity = 'OID' - Open Identity on top of OAuth!

mnemonics.title: Kerberos Tickets
mnemonics.content: TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) → TGS (Ticket Granting Service) → Service Ticket. Remember: 'T'icket 'G'ranting 'S'ervice gives 'T'ickets - TGT then TGS then T!

mnemonics.title: CHAP
mnemonics.content: CHAP = Challenge Handshake Authentication Protocol. Remember: 'C'hallenge 'H'andshake 'A'uthentication 'P'rotocol - Three-way handshake with challenge-response!

mnemonics.title: EAP Methods
mnemonics.content: Common EAP methods: EAP-MD5, EAP-TLS, EAP-TTLS, PEAP. Remember: 'M'D5 'T'LS 'T'TLS 'P'EAP - Most Times TLS Provides Extra Protection!

mnemonics.title: RADIUS
mnemonics.content: RADIUS = Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service. Remember: 'R'emote 'A'uthentication 'D'ial-'I'n 'U'ser 'S'ervice - Dial-in users service remotely!

acronyms.usage: Use when describing authentication requiring two or more verification methods (something you know, have, or are)
Use when describing system allowing users to log in once and access multiple applications
Use when describing temporary passwords valid for single authentication session
Use when describing framework of roles, policies, hardware, software for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption
Use in Kerberos authentication - initial ticket obtained from Authentication Server
Use in Kerberos authentication - service that issues tickets for specific resources
Use when describing authorization framework enabling third-party access without sharing credentials
Use when describing identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 for authentication
Use when describing AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) protocol for network access

acronyms.expansion: Terminal Access Controller Access Control System
acronyms.usage: Use when describing Cisco AAA protocol - TACACS+ is the enhanced version
Use when describing simple authentication protocol sending passwords in plaintext
Use when describing authentication protocol using challenge-response mechanism
Use when describing framework for multiple authentication methods
Use when describing XML-based framework for exchanging authentication and authorization data
Use when describing compact, URL-safe tokens for securely transmitting claims between parties

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a movie theater: You get a 'Ticket Granting Ticket' (movie pass) at the entrance (AS), then exchange it at the box office (TGS) for a specific show ticket (service ticket), and finally enter the theater (resource). Three steps, three tickets!

Picture a hotel key card system: You (user) ask hotel (resource server) for access → Reception (authorization server) gives you a temporary key card (authorization code) → You exchange it at front desk (token endpoint) for your room key (access token) → Now you can enter your room (access resource)!

Think of a safe deposit box: Factor 1 = The key you hold (something you HAVE), Factor 2 = Your PIN code you know (something you KNOW), Factor 3 = Your fingerprint/retina scan (something you ARE). All three needed for maximum security!

Imagine two people meeting and both showing ID cards simultaneously - Client authenticates Server AND Server authenticates Client. Like both police officers showing badges to each other before sharing information!
Picture a chain of command: Root CA (General) → Intermediate CA (Colonel) → End Entity Certificate (Soldier). You trust the soldier because you trust the colonel who was appointed by the general!

Imagine an all-inclusive resort wristband: You authenticate once at check-in (login) → Get wristband (token) → Show wristband at every restaurant/pool/gym (access resources) without showing ID again. Token has expiry time!
Think of face recognition at airport: Your face IS your passport (something you ARE). No passwords to forget, no tokens to lose - but cannot be changed if compromised!

Picture a theme park bracelet: You buy one ticket (authenticate once) → Wear bracelet → Get unlimited access to all rides (applications) for the whole day without re-entering or buying separate tickets!

analogies.analogy: Remote authentication is like banking by phone - you must prove your identity to a distant teller who cannot see you. Local authentication is like walking into a bank branch where the teller can see you in person. Remote requires stronger proof because the verifier cannot physically see you.

Password is like a house key - if someone copies it, they can enter anytime. Token is like a temporary hotel key card - even if stolen, it expires and becomes useless quickly. Tokens are more secure for remote access.

Digital certificates are like government-issued ID cards: They contain your identity information (name, photo), are signed by a trusted authority (government), and can be verified by anyone who trusts that authority. Just as police accept government ID, servers accept certificates signed by trusted CAs.

OAuth is like giving your car to a valet: You keep your car keys (credentials) but hand over a temporary valet key (access token). The valet (third-party app) can park your car (access your data) but cannot open the trunk or drive anywhere else. The valet key limits what they can do.

analogies.concept: Kerberos as a movie theater system
analogies.analogy: Kerberos works like movie theater tickets: The ticket counter (Authentication Server) gives you a universal pass (TGT). When you want to see a specific movie, you exchange that pass at the box office (TGS) for that specific movie ticket. Each ticket is only valid for one movie at one time.

Session tokens are like numbered wristbands at a concert: You show your ticket (authenticate), get a wristband with a number (session token). Staff check the number (validate session) but don't need to see your ID again. When the concert ends (session expires), the wristband becomes useless.

analogies.concept: Public Key Encryption as open lock boxes
analogies.analogy: Public key encryption is like a mailbox with two keys: Everyone has the public key (open lock) to drop letters in, but only the owner has the private key (unique key) to open and read them. You can receive secure messages from anyone, but only you can read them.

analogies.concept: Replay Attack prevention as time-stamped tickets
analogies.analogy: Preventing replay attacks is like concert tickets with dates: Even if someone copies a valid ticket, the date makes it useless after the event. Authentication protocols add timestamps/nonces so stolen credentials cannot be reused by attackers.
